# Transcrição: The_Darkside_of_Doing_What_You_Love

- **Fonte:** The_Darkside_of_Doing_What_You_Love
- **Duração:** 01:29
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 01:29]

There's a really dark side to doing something you love, that I don't think people talk about a lot. We look at entrepreneurs who work 16 hours a day and we praise them because we go, wow, look at that person, they're doing what they love, they're doing what they're passionate about. But I really think behind every entrepreneur is a partner that is sacrificing a lot for that entrepreneur to be doing what they're doing, especially if you have kids. And that person in my family is my wife. I moved to the US back in 2016, 2017, and my son was two and a half months old. And I said to my wife, do you wanna take a risk and go to America? Well, first of all, she's like, yeah, hell yeah, let's do it. I was away 200 days of the year. She was basically like a single mom. And it pains me, man, because I didn't realize that with more success actually requires more sacrifice. And then I want more success. Well, guess what, more sacrifice. I didn't know that because on social media, it looks like, oh, you just, more success is free. Just work hard and you get more success. I didn't see the other side of it. So because I love what I did so much, every time I was working, everyone around me was praising me. And because my wife was so supportive, she was praising me too. But she was actually suffering and hurting inside. She's such a strong woman that she doesn't like to bring it up. But then now as we're growing in our marriage, now she opens up and she shares with me. She goes, you know, those years were really hard. But she goes, but you know what? I made that decision with you. I wouldn't do it differently, but I just want you to know those were the four hardest years of my life. And I just, I didn't see the cost. But now that I see it, I don't want her to do that. So now I have to go, look, I love what I do, Jen, but I love my family a lot more.
